content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
January 27,
1994
Councilmeinber Patricio Miranda
City of Irwindale
5050 North Irwindale Avenue
Irwindale,
CA
91706
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—006
Dear Mr. Miranda:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning your duties under the conflict—of—interest provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
as a member of the
Irwindale Redevelopment Agency Board of Directors.
1
QUESTION
May you participate in decisions of the Irwindale Community
Redevelopment Agency Board of Directors to determine who will pay
the property taxes and maintenance costs on real property
purchased from a private citizen where the property in question is
within 2,500 feet of your real property?
CONCLUSION
You may participate in the decisions so long as the decisions
will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on your real property or your sources of income.
Although it does
not appear that the decisions will have such an effect,
we may not
possess all of the facts necessary to make such a determination.
FACTS
In 1985, the Irwindale Community Redevelopment Agency (the
“Agency”) purchased a piece of residential property from Myra
Garcia.
She was granted a life estate in the property.
The grant
deed on the property conditioned the life estate on the grantee’s
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
.A—94—006
Page
2
payment of the property taxes and maintenance of the property in
tenantable condition.
Contrary to the conditions in the grant
deed, the Board has paid the property taxes and maintenance costs.
The Board of Directors of the Agency will be considering
whether it should continue to pay the property taxes and
maintenance costs on the property.
It may also consider
modification of the conditions in the grant deed.
You are a counciliuember of the City of Irwindale and a member
of the Agency Board of Directors.
You own the following property in the City of Irwindale which
is more than 300 feet but less than 2,500 feet from the Agency
property:
1.
Two parcels on Hidalgo Street
(one is your residence);
and
2.
Commercial property which is leased to a Jack-In-The-Box
restaurant and which is located at Arrow Highway.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
As a member of the
Irwindale City Council and as a member of the Agency Board of
Directors, your are a public official under the Act.
(Section
82048.)
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
2 or on:
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
2
According to our conversation of January 24,
1994, Myra Garcia
is not a member of your immediate family.
(Section 82029.)


Our File No.
A—94--006
Page
3
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Section 87103(b)
and
(C).
Foreseeability
Under the facts presented, the focus of our analysis is
whether the Agency Board of Directors’ decisions concerning the
grantee’s obligations under the grant deed will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on your real property or
sources of income.
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not
required.
However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v.
Downey Community
Development Comm.
(1987)
196 Cal.
App.
3d 983,
989—991; Witt v.
Morrow
(1977)
70 Cal.
App.
3d 817; In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC
Ops.
198.)
On the basis of the facts you have provided, the decisions
before the Agency involve determination of who should pay the
property taxes and maintenance costs.
Absent facts to indicate
there would be financial effects on your property resulting from
these decisions, it does not appear there is a substantial
likelihood that such decisions will affect your economic
interests.
For example, it does not appear that the character of
the neighborhood nor the condition of the property in question
would be affected by these decisions.
If this is the case, it
appears you have no conflict of interest if your participate in
the decisions.
Materiality
However, if additional facts
3 exist which indicate that it is
foreseeable that there may be a material financial effect on your
interests,
you must determine whether the effect will be
material.
4
Since you own several parcels in the jurisdiction, the
relevant materiality regulation would be Regulation 18702.3 with
respect to your real property interests.
The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing
advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops., 71.)
In a previous letter,
we also detailed how you may determine
whether a decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interests.
(Curley Advice Letter,
No. A-93-482.)


Our File No.
A—94—006
Page 4
Regulation 18702.3 provides that the effect of a decision on
real property in which an official has an economic interest is
material if:
(3)
The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is located
within a radius of 2,500 feet of the boundaries
(or
the proposed boundaries) of the property which is
the subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or
more on the fair market value of the real
property in which the official has an
interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of the
property by $1,000 or more per 12 month
period.
Your parcels are within 2,500 feet of the property in
question.
Consequently, if Regulation 18702.3 is applicable,
you
would not be able to participate in the decisions regarding the
payment of taxes and maintenance costs on the property in
question, if the decisions will foreseeably increase or decrease
the fair market value of your real property by $10,000 or more or
the rental value of your property by at least $1,000 in a 12—month
period.
(Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) (A).)
In addition to the above,
your commercial property is leased.
Therefore, your tenants are sources of income to you pursuant to
Section 87103(c).
Regulation 18702.2 provides the standards for
determining materiality where a source of income which is a
business entity is indirectly affected by a governmental decision.
Thus, your disqualification may be required, for example, if
additional facts exist which show that the commercial tenant’s
revenues would be indirectly materially affected by the Agency’s
decisions.


Our File No.
A-94—006
Page 5
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-59O1..
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1Oc per page.
